prompt: Which city in the world do you find most fascinating to think about, and why? Pick one and commit to it.
response:
I’ll commit to **Istanbul**.

It’s the only city that sits on two continents, and that’s not just geography—it’s a living metaphor. Istanbul has been the capital of empires that called themselves Roman, Christian, and Islamic, sometimes all at once. The Hagia Sophia alone has been a cathedral, a mosque, and a museum, depending on who was in charge and what century it was. The Bosporus is a liquid timeline: stand on the Galata Bridge and you can watch Europe on your left and Asia on your right while fishing lines arc overhead like someone’s trying to stitch the two sides together.

What fascinates me most is that Istanbul never really “became” one thing—it refused to finish the sentence. It’s a city that kept every layer of its past audible, like a chord instead of a note. You can hear it in the call to prayer echoing against Byzantine brick, taste it in the coffee that’s brewed the same way in alleyways where Ottoman judges once drank it, feel it in the ferries that still cross the same water where Constantine’s galleys once sailed.

Most cities choose an identity; Istanbul negotiated a permanent truce between them all.
